FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 11-12, N=10,157

FT_IC2.evidence: Online dating app use: 'Have you ever used a dating app?' Qualifies as online dating/hookup app exposure

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable logistic regression with sociodemographic predictors of online dating behavior

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports AOR 2.72 (95% CI 1.11-6.78) for male sex; AOR 12.97 (95% CI 4.32-38.96) for sexual minority status

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD data with inferential statistics

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (BMC Research Notes, 2024), no evidence of duplication

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports cross-sectional analyses. FT_EC7 applies only when ALL analyses involving smartphone/SM as DV are longitudinal/prospective. Cross-sectional design means rule (c) is not met, so FT_EC7 does not apply.
notes: Online dating is the dependent variable; sociodemographic factors are predictors. Cross-sectional design excludes FT_EC7 applicability.

FT_IC1.evidence: Commentary/opinion piece that cites ABCD study findings but does not conduct independent ABCD analyses

FT_IC2.evidence: Discusses problematic smartphone use, which qualifies as a smartphone/SM exposure variable

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical tests reported. This is narrative commentary and opinion only

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficients, test statistics, or p-values reported for statistical associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Commentary/editorial/opinion piece citing Carter et al (2024), not original empirical analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Acta Paediatrica, 2024), appears to be original publication

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC5 not met; document is commentary, not original empirical analysis
notes: Fails on FT_IC5 (not original empirical analysis) and FT_IC3 (no inferential statistics). Excluded at study design level.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from 2019-2020 follow-up, N=10,147, mean age 12.0 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple specific social media and smartphone variables: app use (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, YouTube, Twitter, Facebook, Tumblr), time spent, secret accounts, cyberbullying exposure, problematic/addictive use patterns, parental monitoring

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects models and logistic regression testing associations between digital exposures and BPM Total Problems T-scores and suicide attempt history

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports coefficient estimate=1.78, 95% CI [1.58-1.98], t=17.41, p<0.001 for digital exposomic risk and BPM-T-scores; OR=1.76, 95% CI [1.39-2.23], p<0.001 for suicide attempts

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using exposome-wide association study (ExWAS) methodology on ABCD data with split train/test samples

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (NPP - Digital Psychiatry and Neuroscience, 2024), no evidence of prior publication

FT_EC7_reason: Social media/smartphone use is the independent variable (exposure/predictor), not the dependent variable. FT_EC7 applies only when smartphone/SM is DV in every analysis. Rule (a) not met.
notes: Digital exposures predict mental health outcomes. Multiple SM platforms examined with separately extractable coefficients. Strong methodology with validation sample.
